1: Two ways of life: the blessed man delights in and meditates on the LORD's law, fruitful like a tree planted by water, while the wicked are like chaff, unable to stand in the judgment.
2: A royal, messianic psalm: nations conspire against the LORD and his anointed king, but God installs his Son as king on Zion and warns earth's rulers to submit to him in reverence.
3: Psalm 3 is a lament turned confident testimony: surrounded by enemies who say God will not save him, the psalmist finds he can lie down and sleep in peace, because it is the LORD who sustains him and to whom salvation belongs.
4: An evening lament answering slander and anxiety with trust: the psalmist urges his accusers to be still before God, then lies down and sleeps because the LORD alone keeps him safe.
5: A morning prayer of petition: God takes no pleasure in wickedness, so the psalmist asks to be led in a straight path past deceitful enemies, trusting those who take refuge in him rejoice.
6: A penitential lament: bodily weakness, nightly tears, and anguish meet the psalmist's plea for mercy under God's discipline, turning near the end to assurance that the LORD has heard his weeping.
7: Psalm 7 is an appeal to God as righteous judge: the psalmist protests his innocence of a specific charge and asks that the wicked man's own violence, like one who digs a pit and falls into it himself, recoil on his own head.
8: A hymn of praise over creation: gazing at the heavens, the psalmist marvels that God is mindful of man at all, yet has crowned humanity with glory and given it dominion over creation.
9: Praise joined with confidence in divine justice: the psalmist thanks God for judging the nations and putting down the wicked, and calls the LORD a refuge who has not forgotten the humble.
10: Continuing Psalm 9, a lament over arrogant men who deny God sees or will judge them while preying on the poor; it pleads with the LORD, King forever, to break the oppressor's arm.
11: A short psalm of trust answering the counsel to flee danger: the psalmist rests in the LORD, who reigns from his holy temple, testing both righteous and wicked and loving uprightness.
12: Psalm 12 laments a society where the faithful have grown scarce and flattering, boastful speech prevails, then sets against it the LORD's own words as the reliable counterpoint, pure as silver refined seven times in the furnace.
13: Psalm 13 is a brief lament built on a fourfold 'how long,' moving from raw complaint about God's apparent absence to a deliberate act of trust and a resolve to sing, even before the crisis has actually lifted.
14: A wisdom psalm on universal corruption: the fool denies God in his heart, and the LORD's survey from heaven finds no one who does good, so the psalm longs for salvation from Zion.
15: A wisdom psalm framed as temple-entrance instruction: who may dwell in God's presence? One of ordinary integrities — truthful speech, a kept costly promise, no bribery or slander.
16: A psalm of trust reaching toward resurrection hope: the psalmist rejoices that the LORD is his portion, confident God will not abandon him to the grave nor let his Holy One see corruption.
17: A plea of innocence: the psalmist invites God to test his heart, asks to be kept 'as the apple of the eye' under the shadow of his wings, and looks to being satisfied waking in God's likeness.
18: An extended royal thanksgiving for deliverance in battle: the LORD descends in storm and earthquake to rescue his king from overwhelming enemies, the victory credited entirely to God's mercy.
19: Moves from general to special revelation: the heavens wordlessly declare God's glory, but it is the LORD's law, sweeter than honey, that converts the soul, closing with prayer for hidden faults to be cleansed.
20: A royal intercessory psalm prayed before battle: the people ask the LORD to answer the king from his sanctuary, declaring their trust rests in God's name rather than chariots or horses.
21: Psalm 21 is a royal thanksgiving that pairs with Psalm 20: it celebrates the crown, long life, and gladness God has already given the king, and looks ahead to the LORD finishing the work of subduing his enemies.
22: Moves from forsakenness to public praise: it opens 'My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me,' details mockery and pierced hands and feet, then pivots to vindication — words the Gospels place at the cross.
23: Psalm 23 is a psalm of trust picturing the LORD as a shepherd who provides rest, guides in right paths, and stays present even through the valley of the shadow of death, so that goodness and mercy pursue the psalmist all his days.
24: A kingship psalm shaped around temple procession: the whole earth belongs to the LORD; only clean hands and a pure heart may ascend his hill; the gates lift up for the King of glory to enter.
25: Psalm 25 is an acrostic lament and prayer for guidance: the psalmist asks to be taught God's paths, pleads for the sins of his youth to be forgotten, and rests in the LORD's covenant kindness toward those who fear him.
26: A psalm of personal integrity: the psalmist invites God to examine his heart, keeps distance from evildoers, and grounds confidence not in sinlessness but in love for God's dwelling place.
27: Bold confidence joined with raw longing: it opens with the LORD as light and salvation against any army, pleads 'hide not thy face from me' even if parents forsake him, and closes waiting on the LORD.
28: Psalm 28 turns from urgent lament, asking not to be swept away with the wicked and their false peace, into sudden praise once the psalmist is confident his cry has been heard, closing with intercession for God's people.
29: A praise psalm built on 'the voice of the LORD': a violent storm snaps cedars and shakes the wilderness as an image of divine power, closing quietly with the LORD enthroned, giving his people peace.
30: A thanksgiving for recovery from near-death affliction: the psalmist admits prosperity had bred false security, then celebrates God turning mourning into dancing — weeping lodges a night, joy comes morning.
31: Psalm 31 is a lament that alternates between distress, reproach, failing strength, isolation, and settled trust, including the words 'into thine hand I commit my spirit,' later echoed by Jesus from the cross.
32: A penitential wisdom psalm on confessed sin's relief: the psalmist describes the bodily toll of unconfessed guilt and the forgiveness that follows honest confession, urging hearers not to be stubborn like a horse or mule.
33: A hymn of praise on God's creating word and sovereign rule: the voice that spoke the heavens into being brings nations' counsel to nothing, commending waiting on the LORD over trusting military strength.
34: An acrostic thanksgiving inviting hearers to 'taste and see that the LORD is good': it teaches the fear of the LORD as the path to a good life and promises his nearness to the brokenhearted.
35: An imprecatory lament: betrayed by people he had treated as friends and mourned as family, the psalmist asks God to fight for him like a warrior and let those who rejoiced at his fall be shamed instead.
36: Sets the self-flattering, sleepless scheming of the wicked against the towering scale of God's lovingkindness and faithfulness, a fountain of life for those who shelter under his wings.
37: Psalm 37 is a wisdom psalm answering the old temptation to envy the prospering wicked: it counsels patient trust and quiet good-doing over fretting, promising that the meek, not the aggressive, will finally inherit the land.
38: A penitential lament linking suffering to sin — wounds that fester, bones with no rest, friends who stand aloof — yet ending not in despair but honest confession and a plea for rescue.
39: A meditative lament on life's brevity: resolved first to hold his tongue, the psalmist asks God to show him how few his days are, praying as 'a stranger and a sojourner' for strength before he is gone.
40: Begins as thanksgiving for being lifted from 'a horrible pit': God desires obedience over sacrifice, 'mine ears hast thou opened... I delight to do thy will' — language Hebrews applies to Christ — then turns to urgent prayer.
41: Blesses those who care for the poor, then turns personal: sick and slandered, the psalmist names betrayal by a close friend who 'lifted up his heel against me,' closing Book One with a doxology.
42: Pictures a soul thirsting for God as a deer pants for water, remembering better days of worship while pressed by isolation and taunts, answering its own despair: 'why art thou cast down... hope thou in God.'
43: Psalm 43 continues Psalm 42's lament, asking God to vindicate his cause against an unjust nation and to send light and truth as a guide back to worship at his altar, closing with the same refrain of hope against a downcast soul.
44: A communal lament: Israel recalls how God alone won their land for their ancestors, then protests present defeat and reproach despite keeping covenant, simply crying, 'Arise for our help.'
45: A royal wedding psalm celebrating a king's splendor and victories — 'thy throne, O God, is for ever and ever,' language the New Testament applies to Christ — with counsel to the bride to leave old loyalties.
46: A Zion psalm of trust set against cosmic upheaval: even if the earth gives way and mountains fall into the sea, God remains an unshakeable refuge whose voice, 'Be still, and know that I am God,' stills the nations.
47: Psalm 47 is an enthronement psalm calling all peoples to clap and shout as God 'goes up' to his throne, celebrating his kingship not only over Israel but over every nation on earth.
48: Praises Zion as the city of the great King: enemy kings who approach are struck with terror and flee, and worshippers are told to walk her walls and count her towers to tell the next generation.
49: A wisdom psalm confronting wealth's false security: no one, however rich, can pay a ransom sufficient to escape death, wise and foolish alike ending in the grave — hope rests in God's power to redeem the soul.
50: A covenant lawsuit: God convenes heaven and earth as witnesses against his people — he needs no sacrifices since every creature is his — summoning genuine thanksgiving and rebuking hypocrites who recite his covenant while sinning.
51: Psalm 51 is David's confession after Nathan exposed his sin with Bathsheba, pleading for a clean heart. God does not want sacrifice as a cover for sin, but a broken and contrite heart he will never despise.
52: Psalm 52 confronts a powerful man who boasts in evil and trusts wealth and lies, likely Doeg the Edomite. Against that arrogance the psalmist stands like a green olive tree in God's house, waiting on his name.
53: Psalm 53 restates Psalm 14: the fool denies God in his heart, and God finds none who seek him. It closes longing for salvation to come out of Zion for God's scattered people.
54: Psalm 54 is a brief cry for rescue from betrayers, likely the Ziphites who informed on David. Confidence follows quickly, God is named as helper before deliverance arrives, with a promised freewill offering.
55: Psalm 55 laments betrayal by an intimate friend whose smooth words hid violence. Though longing to flee like a dove, the psalmist resolves to pray morning, noon, and night, casting his burden on the LORD.
56: Psalm 56 is a lament amid enemies who twist his words, met with the resolve 'what time I am afraid, I will trust in thee.' God counts every tear and keeps it in a record.
57: Psalm 57 prays from a den of hostile men, lions with spear-like teeth, while taking refuge under God's wings. The lament turns to praise, resolving to sing at daybreak: 'be thou exalted, O God, above the heavens.'
58: Psalm 58 is an imprecation against corrupt judges who deal violence instead of justice, their venom like a serpent's. It closes: when the wicked are judged, onlookers will conclude there truly is a God who judges the earth.
59: Psalm 59 pleads for deliverance from bloodthirsty men who prowl like snarling dogs, set against Saul's men watching David's house to kill him. It resolves in singing of God's strength and mercy 'in the morning.'
60: Psalm 60 is a communal lament after a military defeat, felt as God shaking the land, followed by his oracle claiming disputed territory as his own. It closes: human help is vain; only God gives victory.
61: Psalm 61 prays 'from the end of the earth' for God to lead him to a rock higher than himself, recalling past shelter under God's wings. It turns to intercede for the king's long life and enduring throne.
62: Psalm 62 meditates on undivided trust: God alone is the psalmist's rock and salvation, while men of every rank weigh less than a breath. It warns against leaning on extortion or growing riches instead of God.
63: Psalm 63 pictures a soul thirsting for God like a traveler in a dry land, remembering his power seen in worship and meditating through the night watches. It is a favorite psalm of longing fulfilled in God's presence.
64: Psalm 64 describes enemies who plot in secret, sharpening tongues like swords and shooting bitter words from ambush. The psalm answers that God will shoot first, turning their schemes back so the righteous are vindicated.
65: Psalm 65 praises God who atones for iniquity, stills the roar of seas and nations, and waters the earth so abundantly that hills and valleys shout for joy at harvest. It joins forgiveness of sin with providence over creation.
66: Psalm 66 calls all the earth to praise, recounting how God turned the sea to dry ground and tested his people like refined silver. The psalmist adds personal testimony, offering vows because God heard his prayer.
67: Psalm 67 asks for God's blessing not for its own sake but so his way may be known on earth and his salvation among all nations. It ties Israel's prosperity to the nations' knowledge of God, a missionary psalm.
68: Psalm 68 traces God's triumphal march from Sinai into his sanctuary, father to the fatherless and defender of widows even as he scatters his enemies. Its ascension imagery, 'led captivity captive,' gives it messianic resonance.
69: Psalm 69 is a lament of sinking in deep waters, bearing reproach for God's sake, given gall and vinegar, language the New Testament applies to Christ's suffering. It swings between imprecation and hope God will save Zion.
70: Psalm 70 is a brief, urgent plea, nearly identical to the close of Psalm 40, asking God to make haste and help against those who mock his distress. Its breathless form models prayer offered in genuine emergency.
71: Psalm 71 is the prayer of a believer grown old, recalling trust in God from birth and pleading not to be cast off as strength fails. He resolves to keep declaring God's power to the next generation as long as he has voice.
72: Psalm 72 prays for the king (at Solomon's succession) to judge the poor with righteousness and receive homage 'from sea to sea.' Its everlasting, blessed reign points beyond any son of David to the Messiah.
73: Psalm 73 wrestles with envy of the wicked's prosperity, admitting his feet had almost slipped until he entered God's sanctuary and understood their coming end. It resolves in pure devotion: 'whom have I in heaven but thee.'
74: Psalm 74 is a communal lament over the desecration of God's sanctuary, enemies hacking down its carved work and burning the place of his name. It appeals to God's ancient victories in creation as grounds to act again.
75: Psalm 75 declares that promotion comes from God alone, not any direction on earth, picturing him holding a cup of judgment the wicked must eventually drain. It closes assured God will lift up the righteous.
76: Psalm 76 celebrates a decisive victory God won for his people, where weapons of war lie broken. It concludes that even human wrath will end up serving God's praise, and calls the nations to bring tribute to the one to be feared.
77: Psalm 77 opens in sleepless distress, wondering aloud whether God's mercy has run out, before deliberately turning to remember his mighty deeds. That remembrance climaxes in the exodus, showing how memory answers present doubt.
78: Psalm 78 is a long historical recital meant to teach the next generation, tracing Israel's cycle of wonders met by forgetfulness and rebellion. It closes narrowing the story to God's choice of Judah, Zion, and David.
79: Psalm 79 mourns the sack of Jerusalem, the temple defiled and God's servants left unburied, asking how long God's anger will burn. It pleads for mercy despite past sins, vowing God's flock will give thanks to all generations.
80: Psalm 80 pictures Israel as a vine God planted, now broken down, pleading three times in refrain, 'turn us again... and cause thy face to shine, and we shall be saved.' It closes looking to 'the son of man' for restoration.
81: Psalm 81 opens as a festival call to sound the trumpet, then shifts to God's own voice recalling how he freed Israel and tested them at Meribah. It ends wistful: how differently things would have gone had they only listened.
82: Psalm 82 pictures God judging the 'gods,' earthly rulers meant to defend the poor but who judged unjustly. Their office doesn't exempt them from mortality, 'ye shall die like men,' and it appeals for God to judge the earth.
83: Psalm 83 names a coalition of hostile nations conspiring to wipe out Israel's name, calling on God to scatter them as he once scattered Midian and Sisera. Its final aim is that all may know God alone is Most High.
84: Psalm 84 is a pilgrim's longing for the courts of the LORD, envying even the sparrow nesting by the altar, one day there better than a thousand elsewhere. It prefers being a doorkeeper in God's house to dwelling with wickedness.
85: Psalm 85 recalls a past act of forgiveness, then pleads for God to revive his people rather than prolong his anger. It resolves in a vivid picture: mercy and truth meeting, righteousness and peace kissing.
86: Psalm 86 is a personal prayer from someone who calls himself poor and needy, appealing to God's readiness to forgive. Its central request, 'unite my heart to fear thy name,' asks for undivided devotion, not just rescue.
87: Psalm 87 exalts Zion as the city God loves above all others, imagining old rivals, Egypt, Babylon, Tyre, registered as if born within her gates. It anticipates God's city gathering peoples from every hostile nation.
88: Psalm 88 is the darkest lament in the Psalter, prayed from the edge of the grave and never turning to praise, ending, 'lover and friend hast thou put far from me.' Unrelieved anguish honestly brought to God is legitimate prayer.
89: Psalm 89 celebrates God's oath that David's throne would endure forever, then laments the anointed king cast off and his crown profaned. It closes suspended between God's faithfulness and present distress: 'how long, LORD.'
90: Psalm 90, attributed to Moses, sets God's eternity against the brevity of human life, which withers like morning grass under sin's weight. Its plea, 'teach us to number our days,' grounds a wise, humble life in mortality.
91: Psalm 91 promises the one who dwells in God's secret place shelter under his wings, safety from terror and pestilence, and angels charged to guard him. It closes with God pledging to answer, deliver, and satisfy him.
92: Psalm 92, a psalm for the sabbath, gives thanks morning and night, contrasting the wicked, who spring up like grass only to be destroyed, with the righteous, who flourish like the palm tree and bear fruit in old age.
93: Psalm 93 is a short enthronement psalm declaring 'the LORD reigneth,' clothed in majesty, his throne established from of old and mightier than the pounding floods of the sea.
94: Psalm 94 appeals to God as the judge to whom vengeance belongs, indicting rulers who slay the widow and fatherless while presuming God does not see. Its logic: the one who formed the ear and eye cannot fail to hear or see.
95: Psalm 95 opens as an exuberant call to worship, come, sing, kneel before our maker, then warns not to harden the heart as Israel did at Meribah, when that generation forfeited entering God's rest.
96: Psalm 96 calls for a 'new song' sung among all the earth, summoning every nation and all creation to rejoice because God comes to judge the world in righteousness.
97: Psalm 97 announces 'the LORD reigneth' with storm-theophany imagery, fire before him and hills melting like wax, shaming all who serve idols. It closes gently: light is sown for the righteous, gladness for the upright.
98: Psalm 98 calls for a 'new song' celebrating a victory God's own arm has won, made known to the nations, picturing creation itself joining the choir. It anticipates God coming to judge the earth with righteousness and equity.
99: Psalm 99 proclaims 'the LORD reigneth,' a holy king in Zion who loves justice. It recalls Moses, Aaron, and Samuel as intercessors he answered, a God who forgives even while holding his people accountable.
100: Psalm 100 is a brief doxology summoning all lands to serve the LORD with gladness and enter his gates with thanksgiving, grounded in the confession that he made us and we are his people, the sheep of his pasture.
101: Psalm 101 is a royal psalm: the king vows to rule his own household with integrity before ruling the nation, barring the deceitful and proud from his table.
102: Psalm 102 is a sufferer's lament, days consumed like smoke, isolated and reproached, that turns to confidence that the LORD who endures forever will rebuild Zion for generations not yet born.
103: Psalm 103 calls the soul to bless the LORD and not forget his benefits: he forgives iniquity, heals disease, and pities his children as a father, though we are but dust. His steadfast love reaches every generation.
104: Psalm 104 is a creation hymn tracing the LORD's handiwork from the heavens to the sea, feeding every creature in season and renewing the earth by his Spirit. The psalmist's own praise joins that ordered, sustained world.
105: Psalm 105 is a historical recital tracing God's covenant faithfulness from Abraham through Joseph and the exodus, retelling Israel's story so worship stays rooted in remembered history, not vague sentiment.
106: Psalm 106 is a communal confession reviewing the same redemptive history from the underside, cataloguing Israel's repeated unbelief and idolatry despite the LORD's patience. It ends pleading for gathering and mercy.
107: Psalm 107 gathers four scenes of desperate people, lost wanderers, prisoners, the sick, and storm-tossed sailors, each crying out and delivered, with a repeated call to thank the LORD for his wondrous works.
108: Psalm 108 joins a resolve to praise God at dawn with a plea for victory over hostile nations, insisting that human strength decides nothing and God's help alone is decisive.
109: Psalm 109 is an imprecatory psalm: a falsely accused man answers lies with prayer rather than retaliation, calling down judgment on his slanderer before pleading for his own rescue for God's name's sake.
110: Psalm 110 is a royal, messianic psalm: the LORD invites David's Lord to sit at his right hand and be a priest forever after Melchizedek's order. It is the most-quoted psalm in the New Testament, applied to Christ.
111: Psalm 111 is an acrostic hymn celebrating the LORD's works as great, faithful, and forever remembered, climaxing in 'the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom.'
112: Psalm 112 is a companion acrostic describing the man who fears the LORD: generous, unafraid of bad news, steady of heart, while the wicked can only gnash their teeth and waste away.
113: Psalm 113 praises the LORD, exalted above every nation yet stooping to notice the poor, lifting the needy from the dust to seat them among princes. His wonder is God's humility, not just his height.
114: Psalm 114 recalls the exodus shaking creation itself, the sea fleeing and mountains skipping like rams before the God of Jacob who turned rock into flowing water.
115: Psalm 115 contrasts the living God, who does as he pleases in heaven, with the mute, blind, deaf idols of the nations, closing with a call for Israel to trust and bless the LORD generation after generation.
116: Psalm 116 is a thanksgiving from someone nearly swallowed by death, who responds not with vague relief but a vow to take up the cup of salvation and pay what he promised before God's people.
117: Psalm 117, the Psalter's shortest psalm, is a two-verse summons for all nations to praise the LORD for his steadfast love and enduring truth — brief, but addressed to the whole earth.
118: Psalm 118 is a thanksgiving processional built on 'his mercy endureth for ever,' moving from personal deliverance to the stone the builders rejected becoming the head of the corner — a line the New Testament applies to Christ.
119: Psalm 119, the long acrostic psalm, meditates on and delights in God's law, statutes, precepts, and word through every season of life. Its length itself argues Scripture repays sustained attention.
120: Psalm 120 opens the Songs of Ascents, the pilgrim collection sung going up to Jerusalem, with a lament over lying lips and life among people who love war when the psalmist longs for peace.
121: Psalm 121 is a psalm about God being our keeper in every circumstance: he neither sleeps nor slumbers, and his care reaches even the smallest needs. It can comfort those wondering whether God is awake, attentive, and guarding them.
122: Psalm 122 is a Song of Ascents of gladness at the invitation to go up to the LORD's house, picturing Jerusalem as a city built for unity and urging prayer for her peace, joining pilgrimage joy with civic responsibility.
123: Psalm 123 is a short Song of Ascents in which the psalmist lifts his eyes to God like a servant watching a master's hand, waiting for mercy amid the scorn of the proud.
124: Psalm 124 is a Song of Ascents of thanksgiving, 'if it had not been the LORD who was on our side,' picturing Israel as a bird escaped from the fowler's snare, crediting every survival to God alone.
125: Psalm 125 is a Song of Ascents of quiet confidence, comparing those who trust the LORD to immovable Mount Zion, and picturing the LORD surrounding his people the way mountains ring Jerusalem.
126: Psalm 126 is a Song of Ascents remembering the joy of a reversed captivity, and turning that memory into a prayer that present tears sown in hardship will yet be reaped as joy.
127: Psalm 127 is a Song of Ascents wisdom psalm: unless the LORD builds the house or guards the city, labor and vigilance are in vain, and children are named an heritage and reward, not a burden.
128: Psalm 128 is a Song of Ascents blessing the one who fears the LORD, picturing a fruitful household and long life spent seeing Jerusalem's good and one's own grandchildren.
129: Psalm 129 is a Song of Ascents recalling Israel's long affliction, plowed upon like a field yet never overcome, and wishing Zion's haters the fate of rooftop grass that withers before harvest.
130: Psalm 130 is a penitential Song of Ascents crying from the depths, confessing none could stand if God marked iniquities, yet trusting his forgiveness and waiting for him as watchmen wait for morning.
131: Psalm 131 is a brief Song of Ascents of humility, renouncing proud ambitions and great matters beyond him, likening his stilled soul to a weaned child resting quietly with its mother.
132: Psalm 132 is a royal, covenantal Song of Ascents recalling David's oath to find a dwelling for the ark, and God's answering promise to establish David's line and make Zion his resting place forever.
133: Psalm 133 is a short Song of Ascents praising brothers dwelling together in unity, compared to fragrant oil on Aaron's head and dew on the mountains — blessing that flows downward and outward.
134: Psalm 134 closes the Songs of Ascents with a brief nighttime call for temple watchmen to lift their hands and bless the LORD, answered by a blessing pronounced back on them from Zion.
135: Psalm 135 praises the LORD's sovereign freedom to do as he pleases in heaven and earth, rehearsing creation and the exodus, and setting his living power against the deadness of the nations' idols.
136: Psalm 136, the Great Hallel, recounts creation and the exodus with every line answered by 'his mercy endureth for ever,' so the whole span of God's works becomes an occasion for unbroken praise.
137: Psalm 137 is an exile lament by the rivers of Babylon, where captors demanded songs of Zion from a people who hung their harps on the willows, ending in a raw imprecation against Jerusalem's destroyers.
138: Psalm 138 gives thanks before earth's rulers for an answered prayer and given strength, marveling that the exalted LORD regards the lowly, and trusting him to complete what concerns the psalmist.
139: Psalm 139 meditates on God's searching knowledge and inescapable presence, from every thought and word to the womb where he was formed, inviting God to search the heart still further.
140: Psalm 140 is a lament pleading for deliverance from violent men who sharpen their tongues like serpents, trusting the LORD as strength of salvation to turn the wicked's mischief back on them.
141: Psalm 141 is an evening prayer asking it rise like incense and lifted hands like sacrifice, for a guard over the mouth and a heart kept from wicked company, even welcoming a righteous man's rebuke.
142: Psalm 142, prayed from a cave, pours out complaint when no one else takes notice, calling God his refuge and portion in the land of the living when every human refuge has failed.
143: Psalm 143 is a penitential lament confessing none could be justified before God, moving from an overwhelmed spirit to a thirsting plea to be taught his will and led by his good Spirit.
144: Psalm 144 blends royal prayer and lament, blessing the LORD who trains the king's hands for battle while marveling God notices man at all, and closing with prayer for a flourishing, safe, provided-for people.
145: Psalm 145 is an acrostic hymn extolling the LORD as king, gracious and compassionate, slow to anger, near to all who call on him in truth — praise passed from generation to generation.
146: Psalm 146 warns against trusting princes, whose plans die with them, and praises the LORD who executes justice for the oppressed, feeds the hungry, and upholds the fatherless and widow.
147: Psalm 147 praises the LORD who rebuilds Jerusalem and gathers her outcasts in the same breath as the God who numbers and names every star, binding broken hearts with the hand that governs the cosmos.
148: Psalm 148 is a creation hymn summoning everything that exists, angels, sun and moon, sea creatures, mountains, kings and commoners, to praise the LORD whose name alone is exalted above earth and heaven.
149: Psalm 149 calls for a new song with dancing and instruments, rejoicing that the LORD delights in his people, and pictures the saints executing the judgment already written against the nations.
150: Psalm 150 is the Psalter's final crescendo, praising God with trumpet, harp, timbrel, strings, and cymbals until the summons widens to everything that has breath — ending in one command: let all that lives praise the LORD.
